title.value: Show HN: Unified workspace where AI knows your business

hits.author: not_wowinter13
hits.title: Show HN: Unified workspace where AI knows your business

story_text.value:
Easy to try even without an account! Would love all feedback.

Hey all, I'm building Meltbox as a better human in the loop system. It's a way to centralize all of the decisions you need to make into context-rich briefs in a system built for fast human review.

[Why]

Something major changed recently. Agents have gotten even better and even faster at building apps / sites / dashboards / etc... and that's changed my development workflow in a major way.

I often have my agents build an entire app/dashboard just to make ONE DECISION.

It's no longer just building the product. It's building throwaway internal apps on the fly to help me build the product.

So I built Meltbox as a home for those things. Some are throwaway briefs while others stick around as custom dashboards.

[How it Works]

- Go to https://meltbox.ai and an immediate workspace is spun up for you - Copy/paste the invite link to your agent (Claude Code / Codex / etc...) - The agent gets instructions on redeeming its access token and accessing your workspace - Ask your agent to push you a brief about any work you're doing - You review the brief in Meltbox while the agent stays connected live to it. - Make decisions, mark it up with comments, and write notes that all flow back to your agent. - Finally, Sign up to claim/save your workspace!

[Challenges]

In a world where it's easy to build anything, I question what it takes to build a SaaS tool worth buying than building yourself. Meltbox has required solving a lot of unique problems, not only in UX taste, but also: - Real time / web socket connections: agents live connected to the platform and your briefs - Secrets management: a secure architecture for managing API keys usable by apps and agents - Security / Permissions: RBAC permissions, content policies, domain safeguards and constant Fable / Sol audits - ...and so many more things.

These are not one-shottable. We're building the loops and burning the tokens to make Meltbox really good.

title.value: Show HN: Meltbox – where your agents send you briefs

story_text.value:
Dotflowy is an infinite, zoomable outliner to help you get thoughts, tasks, and ideas out of your head and onto the page. Organize it later. Or, don't! Plug it into your favorite AI tool.

It's like Workflowy but it's open source and extensible with plugins.

Dotflowy is free to use and upgradable at a monthly subscription for additional features.

https://dotflowy.com

title.value: Show HN: Dotflowy, the open source Workflowy alternative [video]

story_text.value:
Hey all, I thought of this idea today: it would be pretty fun if claude could accrue currency and then spend this currency at a cosmetics shop on its own. The twist: the amount of money it earns it based on your ratings of its work multiplied by the effort (currently just token count but might change this). Claude can decide to spend money on small things or save money for larger purchases like the pirate hat - one of the items that actually changes the way that claude talks to you (Aaargh!). At first I thought this was just a whimsical idea, but then when using it I genuinely gave claude a rating of 0 for a session and it was able to sum up what went wrong and how to improve next time. So I added the ability to incent claude to do better work with the idea that better work = more money = more purchasing power (I know the incentive is a little backwards right now with the token count metric). Right now it's entirely vibe coded for speed/time but if it gains traction I will probably rewrite it by hand, the code is pretty gnarly.

Anyways, just wanted to add some whimsy to your days, hope you enjoy.

title.value: Show HN: Claude Bucks – a wallet for your AI agent

story_text.value:
When I saw the OpenAI Codex video for the (cool-looking) Codex Micro, I looked down at my Virpil CM3 throttle collecting dust.

So I just asked Codex desktop to connect it for me-- and it turns out the latest desktop app has the hotkeys/bindings defined for the Micro already. In a couple of hours I had the core options bound and over the weekend I got to full LED status control.

The code is Windows stuff and probably not the hardware you have on hand, but that almost doesn't matter anymore, right? I'm more sharing as inspiration for people to hack their own devices. Once we see someone else do it, surely connecting to your random ESP32 touch-screen device or some XBox controller will offer you some convenience or novelty.

The biggest things I love for my personal workflow: 1. LED status of my running tasks and clicking them to go straight to the chat when it has a question or is done-- makes the multi-tasking so much easier. 2. Flipping a switch to start dictation and flipping it off when done. I dunno why this is so satisfying, but it is. 3. Having a dial to change reasoning level quickly (in my own harness I moved to multiple buttons since I change it so often), but the dial makes Codex desktop swapping so much more convenient. 4. Dial-select quick responses (e.g. "Make it so.") for all the stuff and common skills I use.

The code itself may not be super useful, but the project might give you or your agent some ideas on ways to connect these things to your own device or secondary machine.

Let me know if you have questions, I just wanted to share in case someone else found it handy.

title.value: Show HN: Use your flight-sim gear as a Codex Micro

story_text.value:
Hi HN! A few friends and I tried playing the video game Artemis (the fully cooperative Spaceship Bridge Simulator) together over LAN. For some reason, we couldn't all connect to the same network, so we decided to try using some free tool that would let us join the same virtual LAN without too much configuration (me and one other person were the only technical ones, and we had to explain it to other people who deliberately don't feel like learning technical stuff). The problem was that since we had different platforms (some on Windows, some on Mac, some on GNU/Linux), we couldn't find common ground, just wasted time. That's where the bet to try rewriting a virtual LAN generator that did more or less the same things came from (with the due/necessary limitations, but that would fulfill our main purpose), and that's how TunD was born:

https://danterolle.github.io/tund/

One machine runs as the hub, the others join as clients, and each gets a 10.9.0.x address. It uses UDP, supports basic IPv4 broadcast, and includes a CLI plus an optional Flutter desktop launcher.

I would love to get feedback both on using it and on the code itself. It is not perfect, but it works well for my use case, and maybe it will work well for others too.

title.value: Show HN: TunD. Make friends' PCs appear on the same VLAN

story_text.value: After 6 years of open source development, Datalevin has now reached 1.0. The goal of the project is to have better ergonomics than a SQL database for OLTP workloads. We have made Datalog practical by being competitive in relational and graph query performance, and by integrating full-text, vector search and document indexing into the same fact-based data model. I am the maintainer and happy to answer any questions.

title.value: Show HN: Datalevin – a simple, fast, and versatile Datalog database

story_text.value:
I know it should be useful to very few people from here, but I'll be happy if it helps one person :)

Collecte MTL is a web app (iOS version waiting for Apple review) to know waste collection schedule at any address on Montreal island.

It uses open data provided by the city (which is great!), and is totally free and without ads.

story_text.value:
With the introduction of AI into our workflow, we started using it in many different ways, and we took different paths in how we use it for sowftware engineering.

Stage, treated AI as a tool to chapter a PR into different sections to make reviewing a large PR easier by chunking it into chapters with AI generated summaries, they realized that code review is the bottleneck now, but their take on how AI can improve or speed up a PR review is incorrect from my perspective.

Adding more AI generated stuff to an already large AI-generated code just makes the review slower and more daunting. but their product inspired me to do it the right way.

Meet Pyor! (pyor.review) Pyor's goal is to use AI to reduce the amount of code required to review, not increase it.

Unlike GitHub, Pyor lists code changes in a chronological order for faster and easier review that's linear to avoid context switching, it also uses AI to group the mechanical noise in a PR so a reviewer skims it and spends real attention only on the changes that matter.

we built Pyor by engineers to help engineers move faster in the AI era, and that's our bet.

title.value: Show HN: Stage is sunestting their product, what now?

story_text.value:
I have created a tool to convert a published article to Word format or eupb or pdf With a comprehensive guide to 100 global publishing platforms divided into specific categories.The tool can convert the article to a professional Word document as well as to another format epub .
However, I encountered a problem when converting to PDF; the first three pages are created blank.Is it better to cancel the command to create a cover page, or is it better to create a small tool to check the file and manually delete the empty pages, then add the tool?Does adding another tool, along with all the existing add-ons, harm it and reduce its speed?Please help and offer your opinion on how to fix this bug in the tool..

title.value: Show HN: How to fix blank pages in a PDF file
